headline: CATTLE COMPLEX LEADS, GRAINS FINISH LIQUIDATION WEEK
subheadline: Feeders run 3.2% higher as livestock breaks four-day slide, grains close week mixed after fund stepping out.
lead: Feeders closed $353.38, up 3.2% in their biggest session gain in three weeks, dragging live cattle $241.68 higher by 1.6% as the livestock complex finally answered the question that's been hanging over the week. The grain liquidation that started Monday finished quietly: corn down a penny to $4.21, beans flat at $11.28, wheat the only winner at $5.83. The funds found their exit door in grains and slammed it shut. Cattle found buyers willing to step up after Wednesday's breakdown.
the_takeaway: Grain liquidation finished, cattle complex found its floor, week's question answered.
teaser: Feeders move higher 3.2% as livestock breaks slide, grain stepping out ends quietly.

one_number.context: Biggest one-day move in three weeks for feeders, breaking four straight sessions of weakness that had traders questioning whether the livestock complex had found support. Live cattle followed with 1.6%, confirming the reversal wasn't just thin trade but actual buying interest stepping in after Wednesday's breakdown tested $240.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called livestock liquidation exhausted, both contracts bouncing hard off Wednesday's lows.
yesterdays_call.note: Feeders led exactly as the reversal scenario required, live cattle confirmed the call.

sections.title: CATTLE COMPLEX GAINS HIGHER
sections.body: Feeders closed $353.38, up 3.2% in their biggest session in three weeks, dragging live cattle $241.68 higher by 1.6% as buyers finally stepped in after Wednesday's breakdown. The **New World screwworm discovery in south Texas** triggered movement restrictions but Agriculture Secretary Rollins said it won't compromise cattle movement, calming disease fears that had been weighing on the complex. Feeders led the bounce exactly like they should when the selling pressure exhausts. Live cattle followed, confirming this wasn't thin trade but real buying interest at these levels.
sections.bottom_line: Liquidation finished, buyers stepped in, feeders leading the way back up.

sections.title: GRAINS CLOSE LIQUIDATION WEEK
sections.body: Corn closed $4.21, down a penny and 0.3% to finish a week that saw the funds rotate out with no drama. Soybeans managed $11.28, flat on the session after giving back 47 cents since Monday's high. Wheat bucked the trend at $5.83, up 2¼ cents as **Oman oil terminal attacks** reminded traders the Middle East isn't calm yet. The grain liquidation that started Monday finished exactly like it should: quietly, with no new selling pressure and no weather premium building yet. Fund rotation complete, new buyers wait for a catalyst.
sections.catalyst: Oman oil terminal attack adds geopolitical premium to wheat
sections.bottom_line: Fund stepping out finished quietly, waiting for new catalyst to drive next move.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: liquidation week ended exactly as called, funds rotated out clean.

sections.title: ENERGY MARKS TIME
sections.body: Crude closed $92.86, down 29 cents as **Oman terminal attacks** added geopolitical risk but couldn't sustain the Iran premium that's been deflating since May. Natural gas fell to $3.31, down 1.1% on storage builds running ahead of seasonal. The energy complex is marking time between the Iran tensions that peaked in April and whatever catalyst comes next. Crude's holding above $90 but the conviction isn't there for another run at $100.
sections.catalyst: Oman oil terminal attack adds risk but fails to sustain premium
sections.bottom_line: Geopolitical risk present but not driving sustained energy rally.

outside_the_pit.title: EU suspends fertilizer tariffs for one year
outside_the_pit.body: European Commission dropped all import tariffs on fertilizer to cut farmer input costs by €60 million annually. The move signals growing recognition that food security requires affordable inputs, not trade protection.

outside_the_pit.title: House passes FY2027 Ag appropriations bill
outside_the_pit.body: The bill cleared 213-210 along party lines Thursday, setting up Senate negotiations on rural development and conservation funding. Partisan split suggests farm bill negotiations will stay messy through election season.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA staff won't relocate, survey shows
outside_the_pit.body: Over 80% of Food and Nutrition Service employees say they won't move if required, according to union survey. The resistance could complicate any administration plans to reorganize USDA operations outside Washington.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / feeder ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: Feeders outrunning live cattle today means the feeder discount is shrinking, exactly what happens when buyers step back into the complex after liquidation. When feeders lead higher, it's usually real money, not just short covering.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis steady, feedlots bidding cattle higher
basis.body: Corn basis holding firm in Ohio and Indiana as ethanol plants maintain grind pace through summer maintenance season. **Cattle basis tightening** in Nebraska and Kansas as feedlots compete for replacement cattle after this week's price break. The livestock basis move confirms the futures bounce has cash market support.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain liquidation finish before weather takes control of the story?
weekly_thread.status_text: Answer: Yes. The grain liquidation finished this week exactly as quiet fund rotation, no weather premium building yet. Weather takes over when prevent-plant deadlines start hitting in two weeks.

the_more_you_know.title: Why feeder cattle leads the livestock complex back up
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 3.2% feeder move higher versus 1.6% live cattle gain shows the replacement cattle market driving the recovery. When feedlots need cattle, they bid feeders first because that's where the supply constraint hits. Live cattle follows because packer demand stays steady, but feeders lead because that's where the marginal buyer has to pay up. The 0.684 ratio tightening to its narrowest in two weeks confirms real buying interest, not just short covering.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn planting above 50% confirms Belt on schedule
Weekly export sales: beans under 200K MT keeps demand story quiet
Iran-Hormuz diplomatic progress: any escalation brings energy premium back

daily_quote.text: Don't count your chickens before they hatch.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA, news wires, industry reports
